skill.name: security-headers
skill.description: Validate HTTP security headers, identify risky browser protections, and recommend safer configurations for web applications.
skill.summary: Audit CSP, HSTS, CORS, and related HTTP security headers with practical remediation guidance.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, network, sensitive file, and blocker patterns, but review found they are documentation examples for defensive HTTP header assessment. The skill contains no executable files, no prompt injection text, and no evidence of secret collection or hidden data exfiltration. Low risk remains because users may run network checks against supplied domains and the document includes attacker examples for education.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Code Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: the repeated Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown inline code and fenced documentation, not executable skill code. Examples include HTTP header names, command examples, and configuration snippets used to teach defensive checks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected locations are Markdown code fences or inline code in a single documentation file. No runnable Ruby, shell script, or dynamic execution path was found.

low_findings.title: Network Calls Are Documentation and Security-Test Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE with residual caution: fetch calls and hardcoded URLs appear in vulnerability demonstrations, scanner examples, and external header-checking tools. They are not executed by the skill, but users should only test domains they are authorized to assess.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code snippets are labeled as vulnerability examples or header scanning commands. They contain attacker domains for illustration, but there is no automation that sends local data externally.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File and Security Keyword Alerts Are Contextual False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: certificate paths are placeholder Nginx configuration values, SAMEORIGIN is an X-Frame-Options value, MD5 appears as an excluded cipher, and sendBeacon is used in an attacker-example snippet. No evidence found of embedded private keys, Windows SAM access, weak cryptography use, or Cobalt Strike behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged strings are visible inside defensive web-security documentation. The reviewed context shows placeholders and negative recommendations, not credential access or malicious tooling.

dangerous_patterns.title: Illustrative Attacker Exfiltration Snippets
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes non-executable examples that show cookie theft or data exfiltration to attacker-controlled domains. This is acceptable security education content, but it should remain clearly framed as attack demonstration material.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples contain real exfiltration patterns, but they are embedded in a defensive guide and are not invoked by any script. The risk is misuse through copying, not hidden execution.

content.value_statement: Missing or weak HTTP security headers expose applications to browser-based attacks. This skill reviews headers, explains risk, and suggests framework-specific fixes.
actual_capabilities: Reviews key headers such as CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, COOP, COEP, and CORP.
Explains missing or weak header risks, including XSS, clickjacking, MIME sniffing, downgrade attacks, and permissive CORS.
Provides implementation guidance for Express.js, Nginx, Apache, Django, Flask, Spring Boot, and Next.js.
Suggests CSP hardening patterns, report-only rollout steps, nonce use, and unsafe directive reduction.
Formats findings into prioritized reports with severity, impact, remediation, and testing guidance.
limitations: It does not execute scans by itself unless the user runs suggested network commands or tools.
It cannot verify production behavior without current response headers from the target application.
Recommended CSP policies may need tuning for application-specific scripts, analytics, and third-party services.
Compliance notes are guidance only and do not replace a formal security audit.

use_cases.title: Pre-launch web application review
use_cases.description: Check response headers before release and generate concrete fixes for missing CSP, HSTS, frame protection, and referrer controls.

use_cases.title: Infrastructure hardening checklist
use_cases.description: Translate security header requirements into Nginx, Apache, or platform configuration updates for staging and production environments.

use_cases.title: Risk-focused security report
use_cases.description: Create a prioritized assessment that explains exploit impact, browser behavior, and remediation steps for stakeholder review.

prompt_templates.title: Review basic headers
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner check for a single site or pasted response headers.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these HTTP response headers for missing security protections. Explain each issue in plain language and list the top fixes first: [paste headers].

prompt_templates.title: Harden framework configuration
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate implementation request for a known stack.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a security header configuration plan for my [framework/server] application. Include CSP, HSTS, frame protection, MIME sniffing, referrer policy, permissions policy, and CORS guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Design a CSP rollout
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced rollout with report-only monitoring and third-party services.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a staged Content Security Policy rollout for an application using [third-party services]. Start with report-only mode, define safe defaults, and show how to reduce unsafe directives over time.

prompt_templates.title: Produce an audit report
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced security review for delivery to engineering or compliance teams.
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a prioritized HTTP security headers audit from these observations: [paste scan results]. Include severity, evidence, impact, recommended configuration, testing steps, and rollout risks.

output_examples.input: Headers show no Content Security Policy and no HSTS.
output_examples.output: The report flags high XSS exposure from missing CSP and downgrade risk from missing HSTS. It recommends a report-only CSP rollout and HTTPS validation before enabling long HSTS max-age.

output_examples.input: A site uses wildcard CORS with credentials and allows framing.
output_examples.output: The assessment identifies cross-origin data exposure and clickjacking risk. It recommends explicit trusted origins, credential review, and frame blocking through CSP frame-ancestors.

output_examples.input: An Express.js application needs production header guidance.
output_examples.output: The response provides an Express-focused hardening checklist covering Helmet settings, CSP nonce strategy, HSTS preload prerequisites, and staging tests before deployment.

best_practices: Start with report-only CSP and review violations before enforcing strict policies.
Test HSTS carefully on all subdomains before enabling includeSubDomains or preload.
Match CORS rules to exact trusted origins and avoid credentials with broad origins.
anti_patterns: Do not copy a generic CSP into production without checking required application resources.
Do not enable HSTS preload until HTTPS is reliable across the whole domain scope.
Do not treat scanner grades as final proof of security without reviewing business context.

faq.question: Can this skill scan a live website?
faq.answer: It can guide live checks using header output or suggested commands. It does not run network scans automatically from the marketplace entry.

faq.question: Which headers does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, CORS, COOP, COEP, and CORP.

faq.question: Can it write server configuration?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides guidance for common stacks such as Express.js, Nginx, Apache, Django, Flask, Spring Boot, and Next.js.

faq.question: Does it guarantee compliance?
faq.answer: No. It supports compliance preparation, but legal or regulatory validation still requires a formal review.

faq.question: Why are attacker examples included?
faq.answer: They explain the risk created by missing headers. They are educational examples and should not be used against unauthorized systems.

faq.question: Is it useful for existing applications?
faq.answer: Yes. It can compare current headers with recommended controls and produce a staged remediation plan.
